title: Duration of an Inverse Floater

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: 5-year inverse floater, face value $100, coupon = (30% - 3r) per year, semiannual payments, flat yield curve y = 7.5%.
rules: Replicate cash flows: long 4 fixed-rate bonds (7.5% coupon, $100 face) - short 3 floating-rate bonds ($100 face).
Floating bond price ~ par at reset; small dollar duration ~ 0.5 * 103.75 / (1 + y/2)^2 ~ $48.19.
Fixed bond dollar duration $D_fixed ~ $410.64 (from sum of weighted PV cash flows).
setup.question: Find the duration D of the inverse floater.

learningObjectives: Decompose an exotic bond into vanilla pieces by replicating cash flows.
Use the additivity of dollar duration across a portfolio.
Understand why floating-rate bonds have very small duration (only the time to next reset).

stages.label: Replicating portfolio
stages.prompt: Coupon = (30% - 3r) % * face = (30 - 3r). What position replicates this?

options.text: Long 4 fixed-rate bonds (7.5% coupon = $7.5/yr each = $30) + short 3 floating-rate bonds (coupon = $r per dollar face, so $3r per $100 face).

stages.explanation: Long 4 fixed bonds provides 4 * $7.5 = $30 fixed coupons. Short 3 floaters subtracts $3r per $100 of face. Net coupon = $30 - $3r, exactly the inverse-floater coupon for $100 face value.

stages.label: Floating-rate dollar duration
stages.prompt: Why is the floating bond's dollar duration so small (only ~$48)?

options.text: Floater resets to PAR at each coupon date, so its value is only sensitive to rates over the SHORT period to the next reset (~6 months).
Floaters have no exposure to rates at all -- duration is exactly 0.
Floaters have huge dollar duration similar to long-maturity fixed bonds.

stages.explanation: A floating bond pays the floating rate r each period and resets to par at each reset date. Between resets, only the short interval (here 0.5 yr) carries duration. Hence small dollar duration, ~0.5 * (next coupon + face) / (1 + y/2)^2.

stages.label: Sum dollar durations
stages.prompt: $D_inverse = 4 * $D_fixed - 3 * $D_floating = 4 * 410.64 - 3 * 48.19 = 1642.56 - 144.57 = $1497.99. Now find the (modified) duration D = $D / P.
stages.explanation: Inverse floater price = 4 * 100 - 3 * 100 = $100 (par). Duration = $1498 / $100 ~ 15. The duration is MUCH bigger than maturity (5 yr) because the inverse floater is LEVERAGED on rates -- 4x long fixed minus 3x short floating amplifies rate sensitivity.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: D ~ 14.98. Mental check: a vanilla 5-yr bond has D ~ 4-5 yr. An inverse floater is much more rate-sensitive because falling rates increase BOTH the fixed-bond price AND the inverse-floater coupon. Lessons: (i) decompose exotic bonds into vanilla pieces, (ii) dollar duration is the additive quantity.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What's the convexity of the inverse floater? (Sum convexities: 4 * C_fixed - 3 * C_floating, then divide by P.)
Why is an inverse floater popular in low-rate environments? (Investors seek yield enhancement when rates are low.)
What happens if r > 10%? (Coupon = 30 - 3r < 0 -- bond pays NEGATIVE coupons, which is usually contractually floored at 0.)
